items.title: SOLID I: Single Responsibility Principle
items.practiceTask: Find your project's largest service class. List every distinct 'reason to change' it has (persistence? formatting? rules? external APIs?). Extract exactly one responsibility into its own class with an interface.

quiz.question: SRP is best stated as:
options: A class should have only one method
A class should have one reason to change
A class should be under 100 lines
Each class needs its own file
quiz.explanation: SRP is about axes of change (stakeholders), not size or method count.

items.title: SOLID II: Open/Closed Principle
items.practiceTask: Find a switch/if-else chain in your codebase that has grown over time (payment types, document types, statuses). Refactor it to strategies resolved via IEnumerable<T> injection.

quiz.question: In ASP.NET Core, registering multiple implementations of IShippingCalculator and injecting IEnumerable<IShippingCalculator> demonstrates OCP because:
options: Adding a carrier means adding a class + registration — no existing code modified
quiz.explanation: Extension happens through new code only; the resolver and existing calculators remain untouched.

items.title: SOLID III: Liskov Substitution Principle
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Liskov Substitution Principle

Any subtype must be usable **wherever its base type is expected, without surprises**. If calling code needs `if (x is SpecificType)` checks or try/catch to survive a subtype — LSP is broken.

## Classic violations

```csharp
public class ReadOnlyRepository : IRepository
{
    public Task AddAsync(Entity e) => throw new NotSupportedException(); // ❌
}
```

A subtype that **throws on a base-class operation** breaks every caller written against the interface. (The famous example: `Square : Rectangle` where setting Width also sets Height — mutating one property has surprise side effects.)

## The contract rules

A subtype must not:
- **Strengthen preconditions** (base accepts any string; subtype throws on lowercase)
- **Weaken postconditions** (base guarantees sorted output; subtype doesn't)
- **Throw new exception types** the base contract doesn't declare
- Surprise: no unexpected side effects

## The real fix: split the interface

```csharp
public interface IReadRepository<T>  { Task<T?> GetAsync(int id); }
public interface IWriteRepository<T> { Task AddAsync(T entity); }
// ReadOnlyRepository implements only IReadRepository — nothing to throw on
```

Not-supported operations mean the type hierarchy is wrong, not that you need a better exception message. LSP violations usually *reveal* an interface that should be split — which is exactly ISP, the next lesson.
items.practiceTask: Search your codebase for 'NotSupportedException' and 'NotImplementedException' in non-abstract classes. Each hit is an LSP smell — fix one by splitting the interface it implements.

quiz.question: A subclass overrides a method to throw NotSupportedException. The cleanest fix is usually:
options: Split the interface so the subtype only implements what it truly supports
quiz.explanation: The hierarchy claims a capability the subtype lacks; narrowing the contract (ISP) fixes the lie.

items.title: SOLID IV: Interface Segregation Principle
items.practiceTask: Find your widest interface (most members). Group its members by which consumers use them, and split it into role interfaces. Keep one implementing class; register it under each interface.

quiz.question: A method only iterates its input. Per ISP, its parameter type should be:
quiz.explanation: Depend on the narrowest contract you use — iteration only needs IEnumerable<T>.

items.title: SOLID V: Dependency Inversion Principle
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Dependency Inversion Principle

High-level policy should not depend on low-level detail; **both depend on abstractions**. The *inversion*: the abstraction is owned by the high-level code, and details plug into it.

## Without DIP

```csharp
public class InvoiceService
{
    private readonly SmtpEmailSender _email = new();   // concrete, constructed inline
    private readonly SqlInvoiceStore _store = new();
}
```

InvoiceService is welded to SMTP and SQL. Unit tests send real emails. Swapping SendGrid = editing the service.

## With DIP

```csharp
public interface IEmailSender { Task SendAsync(string to, string subject, string body); }

public class InvoiceService(IEmailSender email, IInvoiceStore store)  // depends on abstractions
{ /* policy logic only */ }

// Composition root (Program.cs) wires details:
builder.Services.AddScoped<IEmailSender, SmtpEmailSender>();
```

**Key subtlety**: `IEmailSender` belongs conceptually to the *business layer* (it expresses what the business needs), while `SmtpEmailSender` lives in infrastructure. Dependencies point inward — that's Clean/Onion architecture in one sentence.

## DIP ≠ DI

- **DIP** — the principle (depend on abstractions).
- **Dependency Injection** — a technique to achieve it (pass dependencies in).
- **IoC container** — a tool automating the technique (ASP.NET Core's `IServiceCollection`).

You can honor DIP with manual construction, and violate it while using a container (e.g., injecting `AppDbContext` into a domain entity).
items.practiceTask: Pick a service that news up a concrete dependency (new HttpClient(), new SmtpClient(), new SomeRepository()). Define the interface from the consumer's perspective, inject it, and write one unit test with a fake.

quiz.question: The 'inversion' in DIP refers to:
options: Ownership of the abstraction moving to the high-level module, with details depending on it
quiz.explanation: Traditionally high-level code depended on low-level modules; DIP inverts the arrow so infrastructure depends on business-owned abstractions.

items.title: Strategy: Swappable Algorithms
items.practiceTask: Implement a tax calculation with two strategies (domestic/international) selected by customer country, registered in DI, resolved via IEnumerable. Add a unit test per strategy.

quiz.question: Passing IComparer<T> to List<T>.Sort is which pattern?
options: Strategy — the comparison algorithm is swappable
quiz.explanation: Sort defines the invariant algorithm; the injected comparer is the interchangeable piece.

items.title: Factory Method & Simple Factory
items.practiceTask: Create a factory that returns the right file parser (CSV/JSON/XML) based on file extension, with each parser resolved from DI. Replace at least one 'new' scattered across call sites.

quiz.question: IHttpClientFactory exists mainly because:
options: Creating/disposing HttpClient per request exhausts sockets; the factory pools and configures handlers centrally
quiz.explanation: The factory manages handler lifetimes (avoiding socket exhaustion and stale DNS) and centralizes named configuration — classic factory benefits.

items.title: Abstract Factory: Families of Related Objects
items.practiceTask: Model two storage families (Azure: BlobStore+QueueSender vs Local: FileStore+InMemoryQueue) behind an abstract factory, selected by configuration. Confirm consumers can't mix families.

quiz.question: Abstract Factory's key guarantee over separate factories is:
options: Products from the same factory are consistent — no accidental cross-family mixing
quiz.explanation: Because the client holds one factory, every product it creates belongs to the same coherent family.

items.title: Builder: Constructing Complex Objects Step by Step
items.practiceTask: Write a fluent builder for your most constructor-heavy entity and refactor three unit tests to use it. Add validation in Build() that throws on incomplete state.

quiz.question: WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args) ... .Build() demonstrates Builder because:
options: Complex configuration is accumulated in steps, then a finished product is produced by Build()
quiz.explanation: The essence is staged construction with a final assembly step — chaining is just syntax sugar.

items.title: Singleton: The Pattern You Should Rarely Hand-Roll
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Singleton

Ensure one instance, globally accessible. The classic implementation:

```csharp
public sealed class AppCache
{
    private static readonly Lazy<AppCache> _instance = new(() => new AppCache());
    public static AppCache Instance => _instance.Value;
    private AppCache() { }
}
```

`Lazy<T>` gives thread-safe lazy initialization without manual locking.

## Why hand-rolled singletons hurt

- **Hidden dependency**: `AppCache.Instance` inside a method is invisible in the constructor signature — tests can't replace it.
- **Global mutable state**: shared across tests, ordering bugs appear.
- **Lifetime is hard-coded**: can't have per-tenant or per-test instances later.

## The modern answer: container-managed lifetime

```csharp
builder.Services.AddSingleton<IAppCache, AppCache>();
```

Same single instance, but injected — visible, fakeable, and swappable. **Singleton the lifetime, not the pattern.**

## Lifetime safety rules (interview favorite)

| From ↓ can depend on → | Singleton | Scoped | Transient |
|---|---|---|---|
| Singleton | ✅ | ❌ *captive dependency* | ⚠️ becomes effectively singleton |
| Scoped | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |

A singleton injected with a scoped `DbContext` **captures** it forever — one context shared across all requests, concurrency chaos. ASP.NET Core throws for this in development (scope validation) — now you know why.
items.practiceTask: Grep your codebase for '.Instance' static singletons. Convert one to AddSingleton + constructor injection. Then intentionally inject a scoped service into a singleton in a scratch project and observe the scope-validation exception.

quiz.question: A singleton service takes AppDbContext (scoped) in its constructor. The danger is:
options: The context is captured once and shared across all requests — a captive dependency
quiz.explanation: The singleton outlives the scope, freezing one DbContext instance for the app's lifetime — not thread-safe, stale data, disposed-object errors.

items.title: Prototype: Cloning Objects
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Prototype Pattern

Create new objects by **copying an existing one** instead of constructing from scratch — useful when setup is expensive or you need many variations of a baseline.

## Shallow vs deep — the whole difficulty

```csharp
var copy = original with { Name = "Copy" };   // record 'with' = shallow clone
```

**Shallow**: reference-type fields still point at the *same* objects. Mutating `copy.Lines[0]` also mutates `original.Lines[0]` — the classic prototype bug.

**Deep**: recursively copy everything:

```csharp
public Order DeepClone() => new()
{
    Customer = Customer,                         // immutable — safe to share
    Lines = Lines.Select(l => l with { }).ToList(),  // copy each line
    Tags = [.. Tags]
};
```

Serialization round-trip (`JsonSerializer.Serialize/Deserialize`) is a lazy deep clone — fine for tools/tests, wasteful in hot paths.

## Real uses

- **Document/quote templates**: "new invoice from template" = deep clone + tweak
- **Test fixtures**: baseline aggregate, clone per test, mutate freely
- **Configuration variants**: default settings object cloned per tenant
- EF Core note: cloned entities need their keys reset (`Id = 0`) before `Add`, or EF thinks it's the same row

The more your objects are **immutable**, the less deep cloning matters — records with `with` expressions give you cheap safe copies because shared references can't be mutated anyway.
items.practiceTask: Take an entity with a child collection. Clone it with 'with' and demonstrate the shared-list bug by mutating the clone's collection. Then write DeepClone() and prove independence.

quiz.question: record Order { List<OrderLine> Lines } — after copy = original with { }, copy.Lines.Add(x) will:
options: Affect both — 'with' copies the list reference, not the list
quiz.explanation: 'with' performs a shallow copy; both records share one List instance.

items.title: Adapter: Making Incompatible Interfaces Fit
items.practiceTask: Pick one third-party SDK your project calls directly from business code. Define the interface your domain actually needs, write the adapter, and move vendor exceptions/DTO translation inside it.

quiz.question: The main architectural benefit of adapters around third-party SDKs is:
options: Vendor types and exceptions stop at the boundary — swapping vendors touches one class
quiz.explanation: The domain depends on its own port; the adapter is the only file that knows the vendor exists.

items.title: Decorator: Layering Behavior Transparently
items.practiceTask: Write a caching decorator for one of your read-heavy services using IMemoryCache, register it with Scrutor's Decorate (or manually), and verify with logs that the second call skips the database.

quiz.question: Decorator beats subclassing for cross-cutting concerns because:
options: Layers compose at runtime in any order and apply to any implementation of the interface
quiz.explanation: Inheritance fixes behavior at compile time to one concrete parent; decorators wrap any implementation and stack freely.

items.title: Facade: One Simple Door to a Complex Subsystem
items.practiceTask: Find a controller action that orchestrates 3+ services inline. Extract a facade/application service with one intention-revealing method, and slim the controller to model-binding + status codes.

quiz.question: Facade differs from Adapter in that Facade:
options: Changes an interface to match a required contract
Simplifies access to a whole subsystem rather than converting one interface
quiz.explanation: Adapter is shape-conversion for compatibility; Facade is a convenience front door over multiple components.

items.title: Proxy: A Stand-In That Controls Access
items.practiceTask: Enable UseLazyLoadingProxies in a scratch EF Core project, loop over parent entities accessing a navigation property, and count queries with ToQueryString/logging. Then fix it with Include() — you've just debugged N+1.

quiz.question: EF Core lazy-loading proxies cause N+1 problems because:
options: Each navigation-property access inside a loop silently fires its own query
quiz.explanation: The proxy intercepts property access and queries per entity — invisible in code, deadly in loops.

items.title: Composite: Treating Trees Uniformly
items.practiceTask: Model a product bundle structure (bundles containing products and sub-bundles with per-bundle discounts) and compute total price and item count via the uniform interface.

quiz.question: The core benefit of Composite is:
options: Clients call one interface without distinguishing leaf from container — recursion lives in the structure
quiz.explanation: Uniform treatment removes 'is this a group or an item?' branching from every client.

items.title: Bridge & Flyweight: Two Structural Specialists
items.practiceTask: Count the class explosion you'd need for 3 report types × 3 output formats with pure inheritance (9), then sketch the Bridge version (6) in code with one working pair.

quiz.question: Bridge solves which problem?
options: Class-count explosion when a hierarchy varies along two independent dimensions
quiz.explanation: By composing one dimension as an injected abstraction, n×m subclasses become n+m classes.

items.title: Observer: Publish/Subscribe in Process
items.practiceTask: Add an event to one of your services (e.g., EntityChanged) and attach two subscribers: a logger and a cache invalidator. Then demonstrate the leak: keep a scoped subscriber attached to a singleton publisher and explain why it can't be collected.

quiz.question: Why can C# event subscriptions cause memory leaks?
options: The publisher's delegate list holds strong references to subscribers, keeping them alive
quiz.explanation: As long as the publisher lives, every subscribed object is reachable and can't be GC'd until it unsubscribes.

items.title: Mediator & MediatR: Hub-and-Spoke Communication
items.practiceTask: Implement one endpoint two ways in a scratch project: direct service injection vs MediatR command + handler + a logging pipeline behavior. Write down which you'd choose for your current work project and why.

quiz.question: Mediator addresses which coupling problem?
options: N components with N² point-to-point references — replaced by N references to one hub
quiz.explanation: Peers stop knowing each other; the mediator centralizes and routes interactions.

items.title: Command: Requests as Objects
items.practiceTask: Build a tiny cart editor with AddItem/RemoveItem/ChangeQty commands and a CommandHistory supporting Undo/Redo (two stacks). Verify: 3 actions, 2 undos, 1 redo leaves the right state.

quiz.question: Hangfire's BackgroundJob.Enqueue(() => Send(id)) reflects Command because:
options: It runs on a thread pool
The method call is captured as serializable data, storable and executable later elsewhere
quiz.explanation: Turning an invocation into inspectable, persistable data is the essence of Command — enabling queues, retries, and audit.

items.title: Template Method: Fixed Skeleton, Variable Steps
items.practiceTask: Create an abstract importer with the template above and two concrete importers (CSV + JSON) for one of your entities. Confirm the transaction/validation plumbing exists only once.

quiz.question: BackgroundService.ExecuteAsync is Template Method because:
options: The host owns the invariant lifecycle (start/stop/cancellation) while you override just the varying step
quiz.explanation: The framework fixes the skeleton and calls your override at the right point — Hollywood principle: don't call us, we'll call you.

items.title: Chain of Responsibility: The Middleware Pattern
items.practiceTask: Write a custom middleware that logs request duration and short-circuits requests with a banned IP (403). Register it in the right position and explain why that position.

quiz.question: Placing UseAuthorization before UseAuthentication breaks because:
options: Authorization runs before the user identity is established by authentication — chain order defines data flow
quiz.explanation: Each link consumes what earlier links produced; authorization needs the authenticated principal that authentication attaches.

items.title: State: Behavior That Changes with Status
items.practiceTask: Take an entity in your project with a Status enum and at least two if(Status==...) methods. Map its full transition table on paper first, then implement either state classes or a Stateless state machine.

quiz.question: The State pattern's key improvement over status-switches in every method is:
options: Each state's complete behavior lives in one class; adding a state doesn't edit every method
quiz.explanation: Behavior is organized by state rather than scattered across methods — new states extend rather than modify.

items.title: Iterator & IEnumerable: Lazy Sequences
items.practiceTask: Write a yield-based method streaming a large CSV as objects; add a Where + Take pipeline and prove (with a counter in the parser) that only the needed lines were parsed.

quiz.question: An IEnumerable<T> from a yield method is enumerated twice by two foreach loops. What happens?
options: The second loop uses cached results
The generator logic runs again from the start — all work is repeated
quiz.explanation: Iterators are recipes, not results; each enumeration re-executes. Materialize with ToList() if you need to reuse.

items.title: Memento & Visitor: Snapshots and Double Dispatch
items.practiceTask: Implement two visitors (JSON exporter and validation) over a 3-type document element hierarchy. Then rewrite one as a C# switch expression and compare: which change is easier in each version — new element type vs new operation?

quiz.question: Visitor is the right choice when:
options: Types change often but operations are fixed
The type hierarchy is stable and you keep adding new operations over it
quiz.explanation: Visitor trades easy-to-add-operations for hard-to-add-types; EF's ExpressionVisitor over the fixed Expression node set is the canonical fit.

items.title: Repository & Unit of Work: Use, Misuse, and EF Core
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Repository & Unit of Work

**Repository**: collection-like abstraction over data access (`GetById`, `Add`, domain-specific queries). **Unit of Work**: track changes across repositories, commit atomically.

## The uncomfortable truth

**EF Core already implements both.** `DbSet<T>` is a repository; `DbContext.SaveChanges` is a unit of work with change tracking and a transaction. So the common pattern...

```csharp
public class Repository<T> : IRepository<T>   // generic repo wrapping DbSet
{
    public Task<T?> GetByIdAsync(int id) => _db.Set<T>().FindAsync(id).AsTask();
    public IQueryable<T> Query() => _db.Set<T>();   // ← leaks IQueryable anyway!
    ...
}
```

...is an abstraction over an abstraction that usually adds nothing: it leaks `IQueryable` (so callers still write EF-specific queries), blocks EF features (Include, split queries, bulk ops), and its "testability" is illusory — mocking `IQueryable` doesn't test translation to SQL. Integration tests on a real/LocalDB database test what actually runs.

## When a repository IS worth it

✅ **Aggregate-oriented, domain-specific** interfaces in DDD:

```csharp
public interface IOrderRepository
{
    Task<Order?> GetWithLinesAsync(int id);          // encapsulates Include strategy
    Task<List<Order>> GetPendingShipmentAsync();     // named business query
    void Add(Order order);
}
```

Named methods per business need (no IQueryable leak), one place per aggregate for query correctness, swappable persistence in genuinely multi-store domains.

✅ Hiding Dapper/stored-proc hybrid access behind one interface.

❌ Generic `IRepository<T>` with pass-through CRUD over EF — ceremony.

## Verdict for your stack

Inject `AppDbContext` directly in simple services; introduce **specific** repositories where query logic is complex or reused. Never build the generic wrapper 'because pattern'.
items.practiceTask: If your project has a generic IRepository<T>: list what it adds over DbSet<T> honestly. Replace one usage with either direct DbContext or a domain-specific repository with named query methods — whichever the call site actually needs.

quiz.question: A generic Repository<T> exposing IQueryable<T> Query() fails as an abstraction because:
options: Callers still compose provider-specific queries — the persistence details it claimed to hide leak through
quiz.explanation: The abstraction boundary is fictional: swap EF for Dapper and every IQueryable call site breaks.

items.title: Options Pattern: Configuration Done Right
items.practiceTask: Replace raw IConfiguration string access in one of your services with a validated options class using ValidateOnStart. Break the config on purpose and confirm the app fails at startup with a clear message.

quiz.question: A BackgroundService must react to config file changes at runtime. Inject:
options: IOptionsMonitor<T> — live values + OnChange in singletons
quiz.explanation: IOptions is frozen; IOptionsSnapshot is scoped and can't go into a singleton; IOptionsMonitor is the singleton-safe live option.

items.title: Null Object & Result Patterns: Taming Absence and Failure
items.practiceTask: Refactor one service method that throws for a business rule (e.g., 'insufficient stock') to return Result<T>, and map it in the controller to proper HTTP codes. Keep exceptions for genuine infrastructure failures.

quiz.question: Which failure is a good candidate for a Result return instead of an exception?
quiz.explanation: Expected business outcomes belong in the return type; infrastructure and programming errors remain exceptional.

items.title: CQRS: Separate Read and Write Models
items.bodyMarkdown:
# CQRS — Command Query Responsibility Segregation

Split your model in two: **commands** change state (return success/failure), **queries** return data (change nothing). They can use different models, different paths — even different databases.

## Level 1: separate handlers, one database (start here)

```csharp
// Command side — rich domain model, EF change tracking, transactions
public record ApproveInvoiceCommand(int InvoiceId) : IRequest<Result>;

// Query side — no domain model, straight projection to DTOs
public class GetInvoiceListHandler(AppDbContext db)
{
    public Task<List<InvoiceListDto>> Handle(GetInvoiceListQuery q) =>
        db.Invoices.AsNoTracking()
          .Where(i => i.Status == q.Status)
          .Select(i => new InvoiceListDto(i.Id, i.Number, i.Customer.Name, i.Total))
          .ToListAsync();   // Dapper is equally at home here
}
```

Immediate wins with near-zero cost: queries skip change tracking and load only needed columns (`AsNoTracking` + projection — real SQL Server performance gains); write model stays rich without polluting reads; screens stop bending domain entities into view shapes.

## Level 2: separate read models

Denormalized tables/views maintained for hot screens (dashboard aggregates), updated by handlers or events. This is where **eventual consistency** enters: the read model may lag the write by moments — the UI must tolerate it.

## Level 3: separate stores / event sourcing

Writes emit events; projections build read stores (Elastic, Redis, replicas). Powerful, operationally expensive — justified by extreme read/write asymmetry, not fashion.

## Guidance

CQRS ≠ MediatR, ≠ event sourcing, ≠ two databases. It's one decision — *stop forcing reads and writes through one model* — with escalating implementation levels. Most ASP.NET Core apps should live happily at level 1.
items.practiceTask: Take your heaviest list endpoint. Rewrite its data access as a pure query handler: AsNoTracking + Select projection to a purpose-built DTO. Compare the generated SQL (ToQueryString) and payload size with the original include-everything version.

quiz.question: The cheapest genuinely valuable form of CQRS in an ASP.NET Core app is:
options: Separate command/query handlers over one database — queries use AsNoTracking projections
quiz.explanation: Level-1 CQRS delivers cleaner code and faster reads without any consistency or infrastructure cost.

items.title: Resilience Patterns: Retry, Circuit Breaker, Timeout
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Resilience Patterns

Every network call fails eventually. These patterns decide what happens next — in .NET, via **Polly** (now built into `Microsoft.Extensions.Resilience`).

## Retry — for *transient* failures

```csharp
builder.Services.AddHttpClient("catalog")
    .AddStandardResilienceHandler();   // .NET 8+: retry + circuit breaker + timeout preconfigured
```

Rules that separate professionals from incident reports:
- Retry only **transient** errors (timeouts, 502/503/429) — never business errors (400, 404)
- **Exponential backoff + jitter**: 1s, 2s, 4s ± random — synchronized retries from many clients create self-inflicted DDoS waves
- Retried operations must be **idempotent** — retrying a non-idempotent POST can double-charge a customer

## Circuit Breaker — stop hammering a dying dependency

State machine (State pattern in production!):
- **Closed**: normal; failures counted
- **Open**: threshold exceeded → fail *instantly* without calling (protects the dependency AND your thread pool)
- **Half-Open**: after cooldown, let a probe request test recovery

Without it, a slow downstream service consumes all your outgoing sockets/threads and takes *you* down with it — cascading failure.

## Timeout — the non-negotiable

Every external call needs one. Defaults are terrible (HttpClient: 100s). A 30s page waiting on a 100s timeout is an outage in practice.

## Composition order matters

`Timeout(outer) → Retry → CircuitBreaker → Timeout(per-attempt) → call`
Outer timeout caps total time including retries; per-attempt timeout bounds each try; breaker sees individual failures.

SQL Server angle: `EnableRetryOnFailure()` on your DbContext gives transient-fault retry for Azure SQL/failover blips — same pattern, ADO.NET layer.
items.practiceTask: Add AddStandardResilienceHandler to an HttpClient in a scratch app calling a fake flaky endpoint (random 503s). Log each retry and force the circuit open; observe instant failures while open, then half-open recovery.

quiz.question: The circuit breaker's Open state exists to:
options: Fail fast without calling the dependency — protecting it from load and you from resource exhaustion
quiz.explanation: When a dependency is down, continuing to call it wastes your threads/sockets and delays its recovery; failing instantly contains the damage.

quiz.question: Why add jitter to exponential backoff?
options: Without it, all clients retry in synchronized waves, hammering the recovering service simultaneously
quiz.explanation: Randomizing delays de-synchronizes the retry stampede — the 'thundering herd' problem.

items.title: Pattern Selection: Smells → Patterns Cheat Sheet
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Choosing Patterns: Work Backwards from Pain

Patterns are named refactorings for recurring pain. Wrong direction: "where can I use Visitor?" Right direction: "this code hurts — which pattern is the known cure?"

## Smell → Pattern map

| Smell in your code | Reach for |
|---|---|
| Growing switch on type/kind | Strategy (+ factory to select) |
| Growing switch on *status* in many methods | State |
| `new` of concrete classes scattered everywhere | Factory / DI |
| Constructor with 8 parameters, half optional | Builder |
| Same pre/post logic copy-pasted around calls | Decorator (or middleware/behavior) |
| Business code calls vendor SDK types directly | Adapter |
| Controller orchestrates 5 services inline | Facade / application service |
| Everyone references everyone (N²) | Mediator |
| 'Notify these 4 things when X happens' hardcoded | Observer / domain events |
| Need undo, audit trail, queued/delayed execution | Command (+ Memento) |
| Two independent dimensions of variation | Bridge |
| Same algorithm skeleton, differing steps | Template Method (or Strategy) |
| Null checks for optional collaborators everywhere | Null Object |
| Exceptions used for expected business outcomes | Result |
| if/else chain where first match wins | Chain of Responsibility |

## Anti-pattern awareness (equal marks in interviews)

- **Pattern fever**: AbstractSingletonProxyFactoryBean-style layering nobody needed. Every pattern adds indirection — indirection must buy flexibility you'll actually use.
- **Golden hammer**: MediatR/generic-repository/microservices applied to every project regardless of fit.
- **Premature abstraction**: interfaces with exactly one implementation forever, 'just in case'. Rule of three: abstract on the second or third occurrence, not the first.

## The senior answer

When asked "which pattern would you use?", the strongest openers are: "What's varying, and what's stable?" and "How many times has this changed?" Patterns serve change — no change pressure, no pattern needed.
items.practiceTask: Review your current project's git log for the 3 most-edited service classes (git log --format= --name-only | sort | uniq -c). High churn = change pressure. Match each hotspot to a smell in the table and note which pattern (if any) would absorb future changes.

quiz.question: A colleague proposes interfaces + factories for a class that has never changed and has one implementation. Best response:
options: Rule of three: add the abstraction when change pressure is demonstrated, not speculatively
quiz.explanation: Patterns pay for indirection with flexibility; flexibility for changes that never come is pure cost.
